meta.title: Vocational & Technical Aptitude Test
meta.description: A self-reflection test inspired by dimensions used in vocational and technical aptitude assessments like the Differential Aptitude Test (DAT), exploring how you tend to handle mechanical, spatial, clerical, numerical, and hands-on technical tasks across 10 dimensions.

scale.minLabel: Not at all true of you

levels.label: Exceptional Aptitude
levels.desc: You consistently show strong, ready-to-apply skill across mechanical, spatial, numerical, and hands-on tasks — the kind of practical aptitude that trades, technical, and clerical roles rely on.

levels.label: Strong Aptitude
levels.desc: You handle most mechanical, spatial, and technical tasks confidently and accurately. A few specific areas could still use more hands-on practice.

levels.label: Solid Aptitude
levels.desc: You generally perform well on practical and technical tasks, though unfamiliar tools or complex problems can sometimes slow you down.

levels.label: Developing Aptitude
levels.desc: Your vocational skills are inconsistent — strong in some areas, less confident in others. Focused practice in a few key dimensions could help a lot.

levels.label: Emerging Aptitude
levels.desc: Hands-on, mechanical, and technical tasks often feel effortful right now. Small, consistent practice could meaningfully build these skills over time.

levels.label: Needs Foundational Practice
levels.desc: Your answers suggest these vocational and technical skills rarely feel natural to you yet. This is a good starting point for focused, hands-on learning.

questions.text: You can quickly work out which direction each part of a set of meshed gears will move, just by looking at it.
You can quickly work out which direction each part of a pulley and weight system will move, just by looking at it.
You can quickly work out which direction each part of a bicycle's gear and chain system will move, just by looking at it.
You can quickly work out which direction each part of a hydraulic car jack will move, just by looking at it.
You can quickly work out which direction each part of a simple lever and fulcrum will move, just by looking at it.
You can predict what happens to a set of meshed gears if one part speeds up or slows down.
You can predict what happens to a pulley and weight system if one part speeds up or slows down.
You can predict what happens to a bicycle's gear and chain system if one part speeds up or slows down.
You can predict what happens to a hydraulic car jack if one part speeds up or slows down.
You can predict what happens to a simple lever and fulcrum if one part speeds up or slows down.
You can estimate how much force a set of meshed gears needs to lift or move a load.
You can estimate how much force a pulley and weight system needs to lift or move a load.
You can estimate how much force a bicycle's gear and chain system needs to lift or move a load.
You can estimate how much force a hydraulic car jack needs to lift or move a load.
You can estimate how much force a simple lever and fulcrum needs to lift or move a load.
You understand why a set of meshed gears makes a hard task easier, without needing it explained.
You understand why a pulley and weight system makes a hard task easier, without needing it explained.

You understand why a hydraulic car jack makes a hard task easier, without needing it explained.
You understand why a simple lever and fulcrum makes a hard task easier, without needing it explained.

You notice small details in how a simple lever and fulcrum is built that reveal how it will behave.
If a set of meshed gears stopped working, you could usually guess which part is causing the problem just by watching it run.
If a pulley and weight system stopped working, you could usually guess which part is causing the problem just by watching it run.
If a bicycle's gear and chain system stopped working, you could usually guess which part is causing the problem just by watching it run.
If a hydraulic car jack stopped working, you could usually guess which part is causing the problem just by watching it run.
If a simple lever and fulcrum stopped working, you could usually guess which part is causing the problem just by watching it run.
You can picture how a set of meshed gears works clearly enough to explain it to someone else in simple terms.
You can picture how a pulley and weight system works clearly enough to explain it to someone else in simple terms.
You can picture how a bicycle's gear and chain system works clearly enough to explain it to someone else in simple terms.
You can picture how a hydraulic car jack works clearly enough to explain it to someone else in simple terms.
You can picture how a simple lever and fulcrum works clearly enough to explain it to someone else in simple terms.
You find it satisfying to figure out exactly how a set of meshed gears transfers motion from one part to another.
You find it satisfying to figure out exactly how a pulley and weight system transfers motion from one part to another.
You find it satisfying to figure out exactly how a bicycle's gear and chain system transfers motion from one part to another.
You find it satisfying to figure out exactly how a hydraulic car jack transfers motion from one part to another.
You find it satisfying to figure out exactly how a simple lever and fulcrum transfers motion from one part to another.
Diagrams explaining how a set of meshed gears works confuse you more than they help.

You would rather avoid working with a set of meshed gears than try to understand how it operates.
